_provenance: Claude-authored original Kaoyan Part B (paragraph matching), batch A6 REWORKED (completes package A to 50). 7 paragraphs (A-G), each 5-7 long sentences; 5 statements are strong paraphrases. answers = 0-based indices; paras=7. DeepSeek not used.

sets.passage:
[A] As ever-heavier traffic steadily chokes the centres of the world's great cities, a once highly controversial policy tool is now quietly gaining ground and acceptance: that is, simply charging drivers a fee for the privilege of entering the busiest and most crowded streets at the very busiest times of the working day. The idea, known as congestion pricing, was long resisted, but is now spreading.

[B] The immediate and most obvious aim of any such scheme is straightforwardly to reduce the sheer number of cars. By deliberately putting a real and noticeable price on the act of driving into the crowded city centre, these schemes reliably thin out the dense traffic, ease the gridlock that wastes so many hours, and noticeably clear the dirty, unhealthy air that thousands of idling, stationary engines would otherwise foul.

[C] The money that such a charge raises matters a very great deal too, and is often overlooked. The substantial revenue collected from drivers can then be poured directly back into improving buses, trains, trams and protected cycle lanes, thereby giving ordinary people genuine, attractive and affordable alternatives to sitting alone in a private car, which in turn reduces the demand for driving still further.

[D] Critics, however, very reasonably raise the serious issue of fairness. A single flat fee, charged identically to everyone, they argue with some force, inevitably falls hardest of all on poorer drivers, for whom the daily charge represents a real and painful financial burden, while the comfortably wealthy, by sharp contrast, barely even notice the same sum leaving their accounts.

[E] The best-designed schemes answer this powerful objection directly and thoughtfully, with carefully targeted exemptions and generous discounts for exactly those groups most in genuine need, such as local residents, disabled drivers and essential workers, thereby softening the financial blow considerably without abandoning the basic and sound underlying principle of the charge itself.

[F] The accumulated practical evidence, encouragingly, is now genuinely positive. Major cities such as London, Stockholm and Singapore have all measurably and lastingly cut both traffic congestion and air pollution in their centres after introducing well-designed charging schemes, and this has happened despite the fierce and noisy political opposition that very nearly always greets such proposals before they begin.

[G] Pricing the use of the road, it gradually turns out, can actually achieve the very thing that simply building yet more and wider roads has somehow never once managed to do anywhere: namely, to make a hopelessly crowded city move freely once again. The deeper lesson, perhaps, is that on a crowded and warming planet the most precious urban space of all is simply far too valuable to go on giving away, entirely free of charge, to the private motor car.
statements: A single flat fee falls hardest on poorer drivers, while the wealthy barely notice it.
Putting a real price on driving into the centre thins the traffic and clears the air.
Cities such as London, Stockholm and Singapore have lastingly cut congestion after introducing charges.
Revenue raised from the charge can be poured back into buses, trains and cycle lanes.
Targeted exemptions and discounts soften the financial blow for those most in need.
sets.explain_zh: S1→D(统一收费对穷人负担最重) S2→B(对进城驾车定价减车净化空气) S3→F(伦敦/斯德哥尔摩/新加坡治堵成功) S4→C(收入回投公交) S5→E(定向豁免折扣缓解负担)。A/G 未匹配。

sets.passage:
[A] A powerful new biological technology known as CRISPR has, in just a few short years, given scientists the almost god-like power to edit the very code of life itself, the DNA of living things, with a speed, ease and precision that was, until very recently, entirely unimaginable. With that extraordinary new power, however, has come a whole host of genuinely hard and pressing ethical questions.

[B] The most striking part of the tool's broad appeal is its sheer ease of use. CRISPR makes the once painfully slow and difficult business of editing individual genes dramatically cheaper, very much faster, and considerably more accurate than anything that came before it, thereby suddenly opening up the powerful technique to ordinary laboratories and researchers right across the entire world, rather than to a privileged few.

[C] The medical promise held out by all this is genuinely immense and inspiring. A great many serious inherited diseases that are caused by a single, well-understood faulty gene, conditions that were long and despairingly thought to be entirely incurable, might one day before too long actually be corrected and cured at their very root, simply by carefully repairing the tiny genetic error responsible for them.

[D] One particular line, however, is very widely and instinctively felt to be fundamentally different and far more troubling. Deliberately editing the so-called germline cells, the ones that are passed on directly to all future generations, would permanently change not merely one consenting adult patient but every single one of their unborn descendants forever, a serious and irreversible step that a great many thoughtful people consider a clear red line.

[E] Deeper and more unsettling fears follow on directly from this. If such powerful germline edits ever became cheap, safe and routine, many worry, they might before long come to be used not to cure terrible illness at all but instead to design and enhance healthy children to order, thereby dangerously widening still further the already vast gap between the rich and the poor.

[F] Most responsible scientists, for all these weighty reasons, now strongly and publicly urge real caution. They are calling, with growing urgency, for genuine international agreement and shared rules on precisely what should, and what absolutely should not, be attempted with the technology, and they want this firmly settled before the science races recklessly ahead of our collective wisdom.

[G] The profound question that CRISPR ultimately poses to all of us, then, is not only the narrow technical one of what we are now actually able to do, but the far harder moral one of what, as a single human species, we truly ought to do. That second question, unlike the first, can never be settled or answered inside a laboratory alone, but only through a wide and patient public conversation.
statements: Inherited diseases caused by a single faulty gene might one day be corrected at their very root.
CRISPR makes editing individual genes dramatically cheaper, faster and more accurate than before.
Most scientists urge international agreement before the science races recklessly ahead.
Editing the germline cells passed to all future generations is widely seen as a red line.
Routine germline edits might be used to design enhanced children, widening inequality.
sets.explain_zh: S1→C(单基因遗传病有望根治) S2→B(CRISPR更便宜更快更准) S3→F(科学家吁先达国际共识) S4→D(生殖系编辑被视红线) S5→E(常态化或用于定制增强婴儿扩大不平等)。A/G 未匹配。

sets.passage:
[A] The familiar five-day working week has powerfully shaped the rhythm of working life across the world for very nearly a full century now. Yet a small but steadily growing number of forward-looking companies are now seriously testing a genuinely radical alternative to it: offering their employees just four days of work each week in exchange for a full five days' worth of pay.

[B] The early results emerging from these careful experiments have genuinely surprised many of the initial sceptics. In a great many of the trials conducted so far, overall output and productivity have either held perfectly steady or, remarkably, have actually risen, as better-rested and less burnt-out workers waste markedly less time, make fewer mistakes, and focus far more sharply and effectively on the work that genuinely matters most.

[C] The human gains from the shorter week, meanwhile, are clearer and more striking still. Employees working four-day weeks consistently report distinctly better physical and mental health, noticeably lower levels of chronic stress and exhaustion, and a great deal more precious time for their families, for proper sleep, and for the rich and necessary parts of life that lie entirely beyond the office walls.

[D] Yet the appealing model plainly does not fit equally well everywhere. Hospitals, shops, public transport and factories, all of which simply must stay open and staffed for fixed and demanding hours, clearly cannot just close their doors for one extra day each week without going to the considerable expense of hiring substantially more staff to cover the gap.

[E] The deeper and more important lesson, its thoughtful advocates insist, is really all about genuine redesign rather than mere subtraction. A shorter working week truly succeeds, they argue, only when the actual work itself is intelligently rethought and streamlined from the ground up, and emphatically not when five long days' worth of unchanged tasks are simply crammed, stressfully, into four.

[F] There is a real and growing competitive edge to be had as well. Firms that are bold enough to offer an attractive four-day week reliably find that they can attract, recruit and then hold onto exactly the talented and motivated workers they most want, in a tight labour market where such people are increasingly hard to find and to keep.

[G] Whether or not the four-day week eventually becomes the settled norm for everyone, the bold experiment is already usefully forcing a deceptively simple but genuinely important question into the open: just how much of all the work that we currently do is truly and unavoidably necessary? A good deal of it, the experiment quietly hints, may in fact be little more than habit, custom and mere presence at a desk, rather than any genuinely productive effort that the world could not do without.
statements: A shorter week genuinely succeeds only when the work itself is intelligently rethought, not merely compressed.
In many trials, output held steady or actually rose as better-rested workers focused more sharply.
Sectors such as hospitals and shops that must stay open cannot easily close for an extra day.
Employees on a four-day week report better health, less stress and more time for family.
Firms offering a four-day week find it easier to attract and hold onto the workers they want.
sets.explain_zh: S1→E(须重新设计工作而非压缩) S2→B(试验中产出不降反升) S3→D(须营业行业难多歇一天) S4→C(员工更健康少压力多陪家人) S5→F(四天制更易招留人才)。A/G 未匹配。

sets.passage:
[A] In one city after another, rivers and streams that were once buried out of sight in concrete pipes underground, or else simply left to run filthy, polluted and ignored, are now being deliberately and lovingly brought back into the light of day. This growing movement to restore urban waterways is quietly changing how people think about nature in the heart of the city.

[B] The most dramatic and ambitious of these projects actually uncover long-hidden water. Streams and small rivers that were sealed away beneath busy roads and car parks generations ago are now being carefully reopened and 'daylighted', physically returning a lost and forgotten river, with all its movement and life, to the visible surface of the modern city for the first time in a century.

[C] Wildlife very often returns surprisingly quickly along with the restored water. Fish, water birds, dragonflies and countless other insects steadily move back in to colonise the newly restored and replanted banks, together threading a living green-and-blue ribbon of welcome nature right through the dense, grey, built-up heart of the surrounding city, to the delight of residents.

[D] There are solid practical gains to be had as well, beyond mere beauty. A green, freely flowing river noticeably cools the hot surrounding streets on a summer's day through evaporation, and a natural, vegetated channel can soak up and safely hold back floodwater that an old, hard concrete channel would instead have sent rushing dangerously and destructively downstream toward someone else.

[E] Such striking success, however, frequently comes at an unexpected social price. As a grim, forgotten industrial canal is gradually transformed into a pleasant and desirable green riverside walk, the rents and property prices in the immediately surrounding neighbourhood can rise sharply, raising entirely reasonable fears that the very long-time residents who endured the bad years will now be quietly priced out and pushed away.

[F] Lasting and genuine results, in the end, depend heavily upon ordinary people. Restored urban rivers reliably stay clean and healthy over the long term precisely in those places where the local community takes an active, hands-on part in caring for, monitoring and defending them, and they tend instead to slide slowly back into neglect and decline wherever that local involvement is missing.

[G] A city's river, it increasingly seems, can serve as a kind of revealing mirror of the city itself: badly neglected and abused when the city carelessly neglects itself, and lovingly revived and cherished again whenever it finally chooses to care. Where a community learns to see the water flowing through its streets as something precious and genuinely its own, the river flourishes; where it sees instead only an inconvenient drain, the river quietly dies once again.
statements: A green, flowing river cools the surrounding streets and can soak up floodwater.
Streams long sealed beneath roads are being reopened and returned to the city's surface.
Restored rivers stay healthy where the local community actively helps to care for them.
Fish, water birds and insects move back to colonise the newly restored banks.
As a grim canal becomes a pleasant riverside, rising rents may push out long-time residents.
sets.explain_zh: S1→D(流动河降温并防洪) S2→B(被埋溪流重见天日) S3→F(社区参与维护河流才健康) S4→C(鱼鸟昆虫回归河岸) S5→E(运河变宜人推高租金挤走老居民)。A/G 未匹配。

sets.passage:
[A] Beneath our feet, almost entirely unnoticed, lies a vast and teeming living world that we very rarely stop to think about at all. Yet the basic health of the thin layer of soil covering the land may, in the decades ahead, quietly decide whether or not the swelling human population can actually manage to feed itself through the difficult century to come.

[B] Healthy living soil does quiet but genuinely vital work for all of us, day and night. It stores immense quantities of carbon safely underground, it holds and retains precious rainwater in reserve against the times of drought, and it patiently feeds and nourishes the roots of the very crops on which every single one of us, wherever we live, ultimately and completely depends for our daily food.

[C] Modern industrial farming, however, has badly strained and damaged this fragile resource. Decades of heavy mechanical ploughing, the routine drenching of fields with harsh chemical fertilisers and pesticides, and the relentless practice of growing the very same single cash crop on the same exhausted land year after year, have together left a great deal of the world's most important farmland alarmingly thin, eroded and biologically lifeless.

[D] A genuinely different approach, encouragingly, is now slowly spreading among farmers. So-called 'regenerative' farming, which makes careful use of protective cover crops, varied crop rotation and much-reduced ploughing, deliberately aims to patiently rebuild and replenish the living soil over time, treating it as a precious resource to be nurtured rather than as a mere lifeless medium to be mined to exhaustion and abandoned.

[E] The real secret of it all, it turns out, lies in the unseen living community within the earth. A single teaspoon of genuinely healthy soil contains several billion individual microbes, fungi and other tiny organisms, whose ceaseless, invisible and almost entirely unstudied activity is precisely what makes the whole complex underground system actually work and stay fertile.

[F] Real change, however, is frustratingly far from instant. Patiently restoring badly damaged and depleted soil back to genuine health takes many long years of careful work, accumulated local knowledge and sheer stubborn patience, which is an extremely hard demand to make of ordinary farmers who are already under intense and constant economic pressure to produce a profitable harvest right now, this very season.

[G] To truly care for the soil beneath us, in the end, is really to take the long, patient and far-sighted view that all sustainable farming, and indeed the whole vast business of feeding the world, ultimately and inescapably requires of us. It is a view that quietly resists the constant modern pressure for instant results, and insists, against the grain of the age, that some of the most important things of all simply cannot be hurried.
statements: Heavy ploughing, harsh chemicals and single crops grown repeatedly have left much soil thin and lifeless.
Regenerative farming uses cover crops, rotation and reduced ploughing to rebuild the soil over time.
Healthy soil stores carbon, holds water against drought and feeds the crops we all depend on.
A single teaspoon of healthy soil contains several billion active microbes and organisms.
Restoring badly damaged soil to health takes many years of patient work and local knowledge.
sets.explain_zh: S1→C(重耕/化学/连作使土壤贫瘠无生机) S2→D(再生农业用覆盖作物轮作少耕重建土壤) S3→B(健康土壤储碳保水养作物) S4→E(一茶匙健康土壤含数十亿微生物) S5→F(修复受损土壤需多年耐心与知识)。A/G 未匹配。

sets.passage:
[A] When the first free online courses from some of the world's most famous universities suddenly appeared a little over a decade ago, a great many excited commentators confidently predicted that they would rapidly overturn and replace traditional higher education altogether. The reality, as it has actually unfolded since then, has turned out to be considerably more modest, and rather more interesting.

[B] The great original promise of these courses was sheer openness and access. For the very first time in human history, recorded lectures delivered by distinguished professors at famous and exclusive institutions could be watched, completely free of any charge, by absolutely anyone anywhere in the world who happened to have a working internet connection and the curiosity to seek them out.

[C] Genuine flexibility was a large part of their initial appeal as well. Independent learners could now study entirely at their own chosen pace and in their own time, pausing and replaying difficult sections as needed, and conveniently fitting demanding courses around the fixed competing demands of paid jobs and family responsibilities in a way that a rigid physical campus had never once allowed them to.

[D] Yet the actual course completion rates, when the data came in, proved to be dismayingly and consistently low. Deprived of firm external deadlines, of the steady encouragement of fellow classmates, and of the watchful, motivating eye of a real personal tutor, the great majority of those who enthusiastically enrolled at the start simply lost momentum and quietly drifted away long before ever reaching the end.

[E] Access, on closer and more honest inspection, also turned out to be deeply unequal. The very people who might in theory have gained the very most from such free education, the poor and the poorly schooled, were precisely the ones who most often lacked the spare time, the reliable devices, the quiet study space, or simply the hard-won confidence that these demanding courses quietly took for granted.

[F] The courses do, however, suit some particular kinds of learner genuinely well. They tend to richly reward exactly those students who are already self-disciplined and well-motivated, and who arrive already equipped with a solid foundation of prior education on which the valuable new online knowledge can readily and securely build.

[G] Online learning, then, has clearly not replaced or destroyed the traditional university at all, as was once breathlessly predicted, but has instead quietly added one genuinely useful, if distinctly limited, new room to the large and ancient house of education. It serves the already fortunate and well-prepared rather better than the disadvantaged learners it was once breathlessly expected to rescue, and a recorded lecture, however brilliant, has turned out to be only a small part of what a real education genuinely involves.
statements: Deprived of deadlines, classmates and a tutor, most who enrolled drifted away before the end.
Lectures by famous professors could now be watched free by anyone with an internet connection.
The courses richly reward learners who are already self-disciplined and well grounded.
Learners could study at their own pace, fitting demanding courses around jobs and family.
Those who might gain the most often lacked the time, devices, space or confidence required.
sets.explain_zh: S1→D(无截止/同学/导师,多数中途流失) S2→B(名校讲座免费向有网者开放) S3→F(奖励已自律有基础者) S4→C(可自定进度贴合工作家庭) S5→E(最该受益者常缺时间/设备/空间/信心)。A/G 未匹配。

sets.passage:
[A] Of all the many pollutants of busy modern life, there is one that has become so utterly familiar and constant that most of us scarcely consciously notice it at all any more: noise. Yet its accumulating effects on our basic physical and mental health turn out to be considerably more serious and far-reaching than the great majority of people would ever casually assume.

[B] The real damage done by noise reaches well beyond the obvious harm to our hearing. Constant, unwanted background noise measurably raises the body's levels of stress hormones, keeping us in a low but permanent state of alarm, and chronic exposure has now been repeatedly and convincingly linked by researchers to significantly higher rates of heart disease, quite apart from any direct damage to the delicate ears themselves.

[C] It seriously disturbs and degrades the workings of the mind as well. Persistent noise quietly fragments and ruins both our sleep and our daytime concentration, and careful studies have shown that children who happen to live and learn near busy roads, railways or airports tend, on average, to read noticeably less fluently and to remember rather less well than their quieter peers.

[D] Its heavy burden, troublingly, also falls extremely unevenly across society. The very poorest and most disadvantaged neighbourhoods of a city are, again and again, also among the very loudest, crammed up hard against roaring motorways, rattling railway lines, and the relentless overhead flight paths of major airports, so that those who can least escape it suffer the most.

[E] Real relief from all this din, encouragingly, need not actually be especially costly or difficult. Well-placed solid sound barriers, sensibly lower vehicle speed limits, modern quieter road surfaces, and generous planted belts of trees and shrubs can all together meaningfully soften and absorb the worst of the surrounding noise at really quite modest public expense.

[F] Yet noise pollution is, even so, very rarely treated as the serious matter it is. Casually dismissed by officials and the public alike as a mere everyday nuisance rather than as a genuine and measurable hazard to public health, it tends to slip quietly and steadily down the long list of urban problems that are actually thought worth the trouble and money of fixing.

[G] To value and to actively protect quiet, the growing weight of evidence now firmly suggests, is therefore not at all some precious or fussy modern affectation, but rather a perfectly reasonable and even prudent concern for our shared long-term health. A society that learned to treat unwanted noise as seriously as it already treats dirty air or foul water might well discover, somewhat to its own surprise, that a measure of hard-won quiet was among the cheapest and most rewarding of all its many public investments.
statements: Persistent noise fragments sleep and concentration and harms children's reading and memory.
Constant noise raises the body's stress hormones and is linked to higher rates of heart disease.
Dismissed as a mere nuisance rather than a health hazard, noise slips down the list of priorities.
The poorest neighbourhoods are often also the loudest, crammed against motorways and railways.
Sound barriers, lower speed limits and belts of trees can soften the noise at modest expense.
sets.explain_zh: S1→C(持续噪音碎裂睡眠专注、害儿童阅读记忆) S2→B(持续噪音升压力激素、关联心脏病) S3→F(被当小麻烦而被忽视下沉) S4→D(最穷社区往往最吵) S5→E(隔音/限速/绿化低成本降噪)。A/G 未匹配。

sets.passage:
[A] In Japan there is a long-established and gentle practice that is known as 'forest bathing': it is not vigorous exercise, nor brisk hiking, nor a race to any summit, but simply the deliberate act of spending slow, unhurried, attentive time quietly among the trees. A surprising and steadily growing body of serious scientific research now genuinely appears to support its real benefits.

[B] The measurable physical effects of the practice begin with stress and its relief. Spending a stretch of calm time within a forest has now been repeatedly shown, in controlled studies, to noticeably lower the concentration of the body's circulating stress hormones, to slow a fast and anxiously racing heart, and to ease dangerously high blood pressure, all without any conscious effort on the part of the visitor at all.

[C] The simple idea itself, meanwhile, has steadily spread far beyond its original home. Once a rather niche and distinctly local Japanese cultural practice, largely unknown elsewhere, deliberate forest bathing is now being actively studied, recommended and formally promoted by official public health bodies and medical organisations in a great many different countries right around the world.

[D] Part of the genuine restorative effect may, intriguingly, even be directly chemical in nature. Living trees continuously release a whole range of natural airborne compounds into the surrounding forest air, and some careful studies cautiously suggest that breathing these in over time can give a small but real and measurable boost to the strength and activity of the human body's own immune system.

[E] Crowded cities, importantly, can also share at least partly in these real natural gains. Even a modest local public park, a quiet tree-lined residential street, or a small green square appears to offer ordinary stressed city-dwellers at least some genuine portion of the very same deep calm and measurable restoration that a vast, remote wild forest so reliably provides to those who can reach one.

[F] Thoughtful doctors, encouragingly, are increasingly beginning to take real note of all this evidence. In a small but steadily growing number of places around the world, forward-looking physicians now actually write out formal 'nature prescriptions' for suitable patients, explicitly advising and encouraging regular time spent outdoors in green space right alongside their more conventional medical treatments.

[G] The quiet lesson of all this research is gently but genuinely radical: that one of the very simplest, cheapest and most ancient of all available remedies for the stresses of modern life may simply be to step, now and then, unhurried, beneath the trees. It is a remedy that costs almost nothing at all, that requires neither special equipment nor any expertise whatsoever, and that has, in truth, been freely available to human beings for as long as there have been both forests to enter and troubled minds to walk quietly within them.
statements: Living trees release airborne compounds that may give a real boost to the human immune system.
Spending calm time in a forest lowers stress hormones and slows a racing heart.
In a growing number of places, physicians now write formal 'nature prescriptions' for patients.
Once a niche Japanese practice, forest bathing is now promoted by health bodies worldwide.
Even a park or a tree-lined street offers city-dwellers some of a deep forest's calm.
sets.explain_zh: S1→D(树木释放化合物或增强免疫) S2→B(林中静处降压力激素缓心率) S3→F(越来越多医生开自然处方) S4→C(从日本小众到各国卫生机构推广) S5→E(公园林荫道也带来森林般部分宁静)。A/G 未匹配。

sets.passage:
[A] As public alarm over the sheer scale of plastic waste steadily grows ever louder, so-called 'bioplastics', cleverly made from growing plants rather than from crude fossil oil, are very often loudly hailed in the media as the simple and obvious answer to the whole problem. The actual truth of the matter, however, as is so very often the case, turns out to be considerably more tangled and complicated.

[B] The basic appeal of bioplastics is, on the surface, perfectly clear and genuinely attractive. Manufactured from common agricultural crops such as ordinary corn or fast-growing sugarcane, rather than from finite petroleum pumped from the ground, these new plant-based materials appear to offer all the everyday usefulness and convenience of conventional plastic, but without the troubling long-term dependence on dwindling fossil oil reserves.

[C] Some of them, encouragingly, can genuinely break down in the environment, too. Certain particular kinds of bioplastic are indeed properly compostable, meaning that under the right conditions they can helpfully rot and return harmlessly to the soil as natural nutrients, instead of stubbornly lingering on, intact and polluting, for many hundreds of years in the way that ordinary petroleum plastic notoriously does.

[D] There is, unfortunately, a serious and frequently overlooked catch hidden in all this. A great many of the materials that are confidently and reassuringly labelled as 'compostable' will in fact only ever actually break down properly inside special, hot, industrial composting facilities, and they do absolutely nothing useful at all if they are simply dropped, as litter, in an ordinary field, a garden or the open sea.

[E] Worse still, bioplastics can also cause real and damaging confusion. When they are mistakenly mixed in by well-meaning consumers with the ordinary stream of conventional plastic recycling, these chemically different bioplastics can actually contaminate and spoil entire large batches of otherwise perfectly good recyclable material, thereby ending up doing considerably more environmental harm than good despite everyone's best intentions.

[F] The very deepest and most important point of all, meanwhile, is the one most often quietly missed in the excitement. The plain fact remains that simply using a great deal less plastic overall, and carefully reusing as much of what we already have as we possibly can, matters far, far more in the end than merely swapping one convenient throwaway material for another, slightly greener, throwaway material.

[G] Bioplastics may well have some genuine and useful part to play in our future, then, but realistically only ever as one small and modest piece of a very much larger and more fundamental change in how we humans treat and value materials altogether. There is, in the end, no such thing as a truly disposable product on a small and finite planet, however reassuringly green the plant-based material it happens to be made from may at first glance appear to us.
statements: Many plastics labelled 'compostable' will only actually break down inside special industrial facilities.
Made from crops such as corn or sugarcane, bioplastics promise plastic's usefulness without fossil oil.
Using far less plastic and reusing what we have matters far more than merely substituting materials.
Certain bioplastics are genuinely compostable and can return harmlessly to the soil.
Mistakenly mixed into ordinary recycling, bioplastics can contaminate and spoil whole batches.
sets.explain_zh: S1→D(多数'可堆肥'塑料仅在工业设施降解) S2→B(以玉米/甘蔗制成,不依赖化石油) S3→F(减量与重用比替换更根本) S4→C(部分生物塑料可堆肥归土) S5→E(误入普通回收会污染毁整批)。A/G 未匹配。

sets.passage:
[A] Cities all around the world compete fiercely, and at enormous expense, for the right to host the great international sporting events, the Olympic Games and the football World Cups, always promising their citizens that the games will somehow magically transform and renew the host city forever. The actual long-term record of such grand and confident promises, however, is decidedly and instructively mixed.

[B] The basic pitch made to a hopeful population is always essentially one of glorious renewal. A single major sporting event, the citizens are reliably and enthusiastically told by their leaders, will draw the admiring attention of the whole watching world, attract a flood of valuable outside investment into the city, and leave behind, as a permanent legacy, a fine collection of gleaming new stadiums, transport links and other public facilities for all to enjoy.

[C] All too often, however, the expensive new venues end up becoming a heavy and lasting burden rather than a proud legacy. The vast, specialised stadiums and arenas that were hurriedly built at great cost for a single brief tournament can subsequently stand awkwardly half-empty and unused for years, or even decades, afterwards, proving ruinously costly to maintain and stubbornly hard to fill with any paying crowd.

[D] The final bills for the whole enterprise, meanwhile, have a notorious and reliable tendency to balloon alarmingly. The eventual real costs of hosting routinely and dramatically dwarf the modest original budgets that were cheerfully promised at the bidding stage, characteristically leaving ordinary local taxpayers to quietly cover the enormous remaining gap for long years after the cheering crowds have all gone home.

[E] Genuine and lasting benefit, where it does happen, demands real long-term planning. In exactly those cases where new transport systems, housing and public spaces are thoughtfully woven, from the very start, into a city's own real and pre-existing long-term needs, a great event can indeed genuinely help to improve it; but where they are merely bolted on as expensive showpieces, it clearly does not.

[F] A small handful of wiser cities, encouragingly, have actually managed the whole difficult business genuinely well. Some, having deliberately used the powerful momentum and funding of the games to pay for badly needed public transport, to clean up pollution, and to reclaim derelict and contaminated industrial land, were in the end genuinely and lastingly improved by the experience.

[G] The great sporting games, in the final analysis, tend to reward not the boldest, proudest or most extravagant bidder for them at all, but rather, quietly, the single most patient, sober and far-sighted long-term planner. The cities that ultimately gain the most from hosting, in other words, are very often precisely those that needed the games least, and that knew exactly what they truly wanted to get out of them long before the very first medal was ever won.
statements: Vast specialised stadiums built for a single brief tournament can stand half-empty for years afterward.
Citizens are told a major event will attract investment and leave behind gleaming new facilities.
Some wiser cities used the games to fund public transport and reclaim derelict industrial land.
The final costs of hosting routinely dwarf the original budgets, burdening local taxpayers.
Lasting benefit comes only where new transport and housing meet a city's real pre-existing needs.
sets.explain_zh: S1→C(为单届赛事建的专用场馆常年闲置) S2→B(被许诺吸引投资留下新设施) S3→F(部分明智城市借赛事建交通、复垦荒地) S4→D(最终成本远超预算累及纳税人) S5→E(新交通住房贴合城市既有真实需求才长久受益)。A/G 未匹配。
